epigraph.text:
Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you 
will fulfill the law of Christ.
epigraph.quote: We can only see a short distance ahead, but we can see plenty there that needs to be done.

content.text: {We can only see a short distance ahead, but we can see plenty there that needs to be done.}{Alan Turing}

Adding and subtracting polynomials is straightforward once you 
understand the key principle: **combine like terms**. Terms are 
"like" when they have exactly the same variable part.

content.title: Like Terms
content.content:
**Like terms** are terms that have the same 
variables raised to the same powers. Only the coefficients may differ.

content.title: Identifying like terms
content.solution:
- $5x^2$ and $2x^2$ (both have $x^2$)

- $3x$ and $-4x$ (both have $x$)

- $-7$ and $1$ (both are constants)

content.text:
To combine like terms, add their coefficients while keeping the 
variable part unchanged.
To add polynomials, remove parentheses (if any) and combine like terms.

content.title: Adding polynomials vertically
content.solution:
**Vertical method:** Align like terms in columns.
\[
\begin{array}{r}
 5x^3 - 2x^2 \phantom{{} - x} + 4 \\
 + \phantom{5x^3 - {}} 3x^2 - x - 6 \\
 \hline
 5x^3 + x^2 - x - 2
\end{array}
\]

content.text:
To subtract polynomials, distribute the negative sign (change the sign 
of every term in the polynomial being subtracted), then combine like 
terms.

content.title: Distribute the Negative Sign
content.content:
A common error is to change only the first sign when subtracting. 
Remember: every term in the second polynomial changes sign!
\[
 -(2x^2 - 4x + 1) = -2x^2 + 4x - 1
\]

content.title: Polynomial application
content.problem:
The revenue and cost functions for a business are:
$$
 R(x) &= 50x - 0.1x^2 \\
 C(x) &= 200 + 10x
$$
where $x$ is the number of items sold. Find the profit function 
$P(x) = R(x) - C(x)$.

marginNotes.text:
So in Christ we, though many, form one 
body, and each member belongs to all the others.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
The heart has its reasons which reason 
knows not.
